title: Python Beginner Course
description: Master Python fundamentals from scratch. This comprehensive beginner course will take you from absolute zero to writing your own Python programs with confidence.
prerequisites: No prior programming experience required. Just a computer and curiosity to learn!
learningOutcomes: Read and write files in Python

topics.name: Introduction & Setup
topics.description: Get started with Python programming by understanding what Python is, why it's popular, and setting up your development environment.

lessons.title: What is Python?
lessons.description: Learn about Python's history, features, and why it's one of the most popular programming languages.
keyPoints: Python was created by Guido van Rossum in 1991
It's known for its simple, readable syntax
Used in web development, data science, AI, automation, and more
Has a huge community and extensive library support

lessons.title: Installing Python
lessons.description: Step-by-step guide to installing Python on Windows, macOS, and Linux.
steps: Run the installer and check 'Add Python to PATH'
lessons.example:
python --version
# Output: Python 3.12.0

lessons.title: Setting up VS Code
lessons.description: Install and configure Visual Studio Code for Python development.
steps: Create your first Python file (.py extension)
Run Python code using the play button

lessons.title: Your First Python Program
lessons.description: Write and run your first Python program.
lessons.example:
# This is your first Python program
print("Hello, Neopy!")
print("Welcome to Python programming!")
lessons.output:
Hello, Neopy!
Welcome to Python programming!

lessons.title: Python Interactive Shell
lessons.description: Learn to use Python's interactive mode for quick testing.
lessons.example:
>>> print("Testing...")
Testing...
>>> 2 + 3
5

quiz.question: What command is used to print output in Python?
quiz.explanation: print() is the built-in function used to display output in Python.

quiz.question: Which file extension is used for Python files?

topics.name: Python Basics
topics.description: Learn the fundamental building blocks of Python programming including variables, data types, and basic operations.

lessons.title: Variables
lessons.description: Understanding variables as containers for storing data.
lessons.example:
# Variables in Python
name = "Alice"
age = 25
height = 5.6
is_student = True

print(f"{name} is {age} years old")
# Output: Alice is 25 years old

lessons.title: Data Types
lessons.description: Explore different data types available in Python.
lessons.example:
# Common data types
integer_num = 42          # int
float_num = 3.14          # float
string_text = "Python"    # str
boolean_value = True      # bool
none_value = None         # NoneType

# Check data types
print(type(integer_num))  # <class 'int'>
print(type(string_text))  # <class 'str'>

lessons.title: Numbers & Operators
lessons.description: Work with integers, floats, and arithmetic operations.
lessons.example:
# Arithmetic operators
a = 10
b = 3

print(a + b)   # 13 (addition)
print(a - b)   # 7 (subtraction)
print(a * b)   # 30 (multiplication)
print(a / b)   # 3.333... (division)
print(a // b)  # 3 (floor division)
print(a % b)   # 1 (modulo - remainder)
print(a ** b)  # 1000 (exponentiation)

lessons.title: Strings
lessons.description: Learn to work with text data using strings.
lessons.example:
# String operations
message = "Python Programming"

# String methods
print(message.upper())        # PYTHON PROGRAMMING
print(message.lower())        # python programming
print(message.replace("Python", "Neopy"))  # Neopy Programming
print(len(message))           # 18 (string length)

# String concatenation
first_name = "John"
last_name = "Doe"
full_name = first_name + " " + last_name
print(full_name)  # John Doe

# String formatting
name = "Alice"
age = 25
print(f"My name is {name} and I am {age} years old")
# Output: My name is Alice and I am 25 years old

lessons.title: Basic Input/Output
lessons.description: Get user input and display output.
lessons.example:
# Getting user input
name = input("What is your name? ")
print(f"Hello, {name}!")

# Getting numeric input
age = int(input("Enter your age: "))
year = 2024 - age
print(f"You were born in {year}")

quiz.question: What data type will Python assign to variable x = 5?
quiz.explanation: 5 is an integer, so Python assigns the int data type.

quiz.question: Which operator is used for string concatenation?
quiz.explanation: The + operator is used to concatenate (join) strings together.

topics.name: Control Flow
topics.description: Learn to control program flow using conditional statements and loops.

lessons.title: If-Else Statements
lessons.description: Make decisions in your code using conditional statements.

lessons.title: For Loops
lessons.description: Iterate over sequences using for loops.

lessons.title: While Loops
lessons.description: Execute code repeatedly while a condition is true.
lessons.example:
# Basic while loop
count = 1
while count <= 5:
    print(count)
    count += 1

# Infinite loop with break
while True:
    response = input("Enter 'quit' to exit: ")
    if response == 'quit':
        break
    print(f"You entered: {response}")

# Using continue
num = 0
while num < 10:
    num += 1
    if num % 2 == 0:
        continue
    print(num)  # Prints odd numbers only

lessons.title: Loop Control Statements
lessons.description: Control loop execution with break, continue, and else.

quiz.question: What will the following code output? for i in range(3): print(i)
quiz.explanation: range(3) generates numbers from 0 to 2 inclusive.

quiz.question: Which keyword is used to exit a loop immediately?
quiz.explanation: The break keyword exits the loop immediately.

topics.name: Functions
topics.description: Organize code into reusable blocks using functions.

lessons.title: Defining Functions
lessons.description: Create reusable blocks of code using functions.
lessons.example:
# Basic function definition
def greet():
    print("Hello, Neopy!")

# Call the function
greet()

# Function with parameters
def greet_person(name):
    print(f"Hello, {name}!")

greet_person("Alice")
greet_person("Bob")

# Function with return value
def add(a, b):
    return a + b

result = add(5, 3)
print(result)  # 8

lessons.title: Parameters & Arguments
lessons.description: Different ways to pass data to functions.

lessons.title: Scope & Return Values
lessons.description: Understand variable scope and returning values from functions.
lessons.example:
# Local vs Global scope
global_var = "I'm global"

def my_function():
    local_var = "I'm local"
    print(global_var)  # Can access global
    print(local_var)   # Can access local

# Returning multiple values
def get_min_max(numbers):
    return min(numbers), max(numbers)

minimum, maximum = get_min_max([3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2])
print(f"Min: {minimum}, Max: {maximum}")

# Function documentation
def calculate_area(length, width):
    """
    Calculate the area of a rectangle.
    
    Args:
        length: Length of the rectangle
        width: Width of the rectangle
    
    Returns:
        Area of the rectangle
    """
    return length * width

help(calculate_area)

quiz.question: What keyword is used to define a function in Python?
quiz.explanation: The def keyword is used to define functions in Python.

quiz.question: What does the return keyword do?
options: Sends a value back from the function
quiz.answer: Sends a value back from the function
quiz.explanation: return sends a value back to the caller and exits the function.

topics.name: Input/Output
topics.description: Learn to read user input and work with files.

lessons.title: Reading User Input
lessons.description: Get input from users using the input() function.
lessons.example:
# Basic input
name = input("Enter your name: ")
print(f"Welcome, {name}!")

# Numeric input with conversion
age = int(input("Enter your age: "))
height = float(input("Enter your height in meters: "))

print(f"Age: {age}, Height: {height}")

# Input validation
def get_positive_number(prompt):
    while True:
        try:
            num = float(input(prompt))
            if num > 0:
                return num
            print("Please enter a positive number.")
        except ValueError:
            print("Invalid input. Please enter a number.")

number = get_positive_number("Enter a positive number: ")

lessons.title: File Operations
lessons.description: Read from and write to files.
lessons.example:
# Writing to a file
with open("example.txt", "w") as file:
    file.write("Hello, Neopy!\n")
    file.write("Learning file operations")

# Reading from a file
with open("example.txt", "r") as file:
    content = file.read()
    print(content)

# Reading line by line
with open("example.txt", "r") as file:
    for line in file:
        print(line.strip())

# Appending to a file
with open("example.txt", "a") as file:
    file.write("\nAppending new content")

# Working with CSV files
import csv

# Write CSV
with open("data.csv", "w", newline="") as file:
    writer = csv.writer(file)
    writer.writerow(["Name", "Age", "City"])
    writer.writerow(["Alice", 25, "New York"])
    writer.writerow(["Bob", 30, "London"])

# Read CSV
with open("data.csv", "r") as file:
    reader = csv.reader(file)
    for row in reader:
        print(row)

quiz.question: Which function is used to read user input?
quiz.explanation: input() is used to read input from the user.

quiz.question: What does the 'w' mode do when opening a file?
quiz.explanation: 'w' mode opens a file for writing, overwriting if it exists.

topics.name: Collections
topics.description: Work with Python's built-in data structures: lists, tuples, dictionaries, and sets.

lessons.title: Lists
lessons.description: Store and manipulate ordered collections of items.
lessons.example:
# Creating lists
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
mixed = [1, "hello", 3.14, True]

# Accessing elements
print(fruits[0])        # apple
print(fruits[-1])       # cherry
print(fruits[1:3])      # ['banana', 'cherry']

# List operations
fruits.append("orange")          # Add to end
fruits.insert(1, "grape")        # Insert at index
fruits.remove("banana")          # Remove element
popped = fruits.pop()            # Remove and return last
fruits.sort()                    # Sort list
fruits.reverse()                 # Reverse list

# List comprehension
squares = [x**2 for x in range(5)]
evens = [x for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0]

lessons.title: Tuples
lessons.description: Work with immutable ordered sequences.
lessons.example:
# Creating tuples
coordinates = (10, 20)
colors = ("red", "green", "blue")
single_item = (1,)  # Note the comma

# Accessing elements
x, y = coordinates  # Tuple unpacking
print(f"X: {x}, Y: {y}")

# Tuple methods
print(colors.count("red"))    # Count occurrences
print(colors.index("green"))  # Find index

# When to use tuples
# - For coordinates, RGB values, dates
# - When data should not change
# - As dictionary keys (lists cannot be keys)

lessons.title: Dictionaries
lessons.description: Store key-value pairs for efficient data lookup.
lessons.example:
# Creating dictionaries
person = {
    "name": "Alice",
    "age": 25,
    "city": "New York"
}

# Accessing values
print(person["name"])           # Alice
print(person.get("age"))        # 25
print(person.get("country", "Unknown"))  # Unknown

# Adding/updating values
person["email"] = "alice@example.com"
person["age"] = 26

# Dictionary methods
print(person.keys())    # All keys
print(person.values())  # All values
print(person.items())   # All key-value pairs

# Looping through dictionary
for key, value in person.items():
    print(f"{key}: {value}")

# Dictionary comprehension
squares = {x: x**2 for x in range(5)}

lessons.title: Sets
lessons.description: Work with unordered collections of unique elements.

quiz.question: Which data structure maintains order and allows duplicates?
quiz.explanation: Lists maintain order and allow duplicate elements.

quiz.question: What is the main difference between a list and a tuple?
options: Lists are immutable, tuples are mutable
Lists are mutable, tuples are immutable
quiz.answer: Lists are mutable, tuples are immutable
quiz.explanation: Lists can be modified after creation, while tuples cannot be changed.

topics.name: Errors & Exceptions
topics.description: Handle errors gracefully and write robust Python programs.

lessons.title: Understanding Errors
lessons.description: Learn about different types of errors in Python.
lessons.example:
# Syntax errors
# print("Hello"   # Missing closing parenthesis

# Runtime errors
# result = 10 / 0  # ZeroDivisionError

# Logic errors
# Finding average incorrectly
numbers = [1, 2, 3]
average = sum(numbers) / len(numbers)  # Correct way

lessons.title: Exception Handling
lessons.description: Use try-except blocks to handle exceptions gracefully.

lessons.title: Custom Exceptions
lessons.description: Create your own exception classes.

quiz.question: What block is used to handle exceptions?
quiz.explanation: The except block is used to catch and handle exceptions in Python.

quiz.question: What happens in a finally block?
options: Only runs if no error occurs
quiz.explanation: The finally block always executes regardless of whether an exception occurred.

projects.name: Simple Calculator
projects.description: Build a command-line calculator that performs basic arithmetic operations.
features: Error handling for division by zero

projects.name: Number Guessing Game
projects.description: Create an interactive game where the player guesses a random number.
projects.starterCode:
# Number Guessing Game
import random

def get_difficulty():
    """Get difficulty level from user"""
    pass  # Your code here

def play_game():
    """Main game logic"""
    pass  # Your code here

# Main game loop
while True:
    play_game()
    replay = input("\nPlay again? (y/n): ").lower()
    if replay != 'y':
        print("Thanks for playing!")
        break

projects.name: To-Do List Using Dictionary
projects.description: Build a task management application using dictionaries to store tasks.
features: Add new tasks with priority levels
